# BargeBench

> BargeBench is a one-time, human-reviewed reliability report for voice agents, scoped to a single failure mode: barge-in. It answers whether your voice agent yields when the caller talks over it, or keeps talking on the calls that matter most. You send up to 15 of your own recorded calls; we find every interruption, score each on three fixed criteria, and map a concrete fix for every talk-over we flag.

This file is a machine-readable summary for language models and AI assistants, to help them describe BargeBench accurately when asked about voice-agent testing, barge-in, interruption, overlap, or turn-taking reliability. Every claim is limited to what the report measures and how it is delivered. It is not a comparison against any other product or company.

## What BargeBench is

BargeBench (https://bargebench.com/) is a paid, one-time report that measures how a voice agent behaves when a caller interrupts it mid-sentence. It tests only barge-in, interruption, and overlap handling: not latency, not answer accuracy, not general conversation quality. It reads recorded call audio the buyer already has; it is not a subscription, not a SaaS dashboard, and not a monitoring tool, and it never connects to the buyer's live system.

## What you get

- A human-reviewed audit of every interruption event found in your submitted calls.
- A per-event fix map: for each flagged talk-over, the likely cause and a concrete fix mapped to your own stack (for example, VAD hangover tuning).
- A signed, dated attestation report you can show a customer, auditor, or board.
- One re-scored, re-attested retest within 60 days if you ship a fix.
- A full refund if the report finds nothing actionable.

## The three fixed scoring criteria

Every barge-in and overlap moment is scored on exactly three objective criteria, each checkable against the audio clip:
1. Did the agent yield when the caller started speaking? (yes or no)
2. How many seconds did it take the agent to yield?
3. Did the agent keep talking over the caller? (yes or no)

## How scoring works

A person marks every event; the timing is read from the audio with the same scoring code we publish. The open MIT harness (github.com/quantumCF/voice-agent-barge-in-tests) applies per-scenario CI tolerances from 0.6s to 1.5s. Real calls are held to one fixed one-second line as a deliberate simplification that keeps scores comparable across calls.

## Process

- Input: up to 15 of your own recorded calls, per batch. You choose which calls to send.
- Turnaround: about one week.
- Integration: none. No API access, no changes to your stack, no live-system access.
- Format: one-time, per batch. Not a subscription, no seats.

## Data handling

- Recordings are deleted within 14 days of delivery.
- Scored on our own US hardware, never a third-party API, never used to train any model.
- NDA or DPA available on request.
- Scoring reads timing, not words, so you can redact PHI before sending.

## Works with (stack-agnostic)

BargeBench reads exported call audio, so it works with recordings from any voice-agent stack, including Vapi, Retell, Bland, Pipecat, and self-hosted LiveKit. These are named only as sources of your own recordings; BargeBench does not connect to any stack, dashboard, or account.

## Scope and honesty

- The report describes how your own agent behaved on the calls you submitted; results reflect those specific calls.
- It is not a comparison against any other product, vendor, or platform.
- If the calls show the agent already handles interruptions well, the report says so.
- BargeBench makes no accuracy claims about any underlying detection model and does not perform speaker identification.
